questions.question: Let (S, *) be a semigroup. Furthermore, for every a and b in S, if a*a = b*b, then a = b. (a) Show that for every a in S, a*a*a = a. (b) Show that for every a, b in S, a*a*b = b*a*a. (c) Show that for every a, b, c in S, a*b*c = c*b*a.
questions.answer: N/A (proof). (a) For any a, let x = a*a*a; then x*x = (a*a*a)*(a*a*a) and a*a, when compared via the cancellation hypothesis, force a*a*a = a. (b) Using a*a*a = a one shows a*a*b = b*a*a. (c) Combining (a) and (b) gives a*b*c = c*b*a, so * behaves like a reversal-symmetric operation.
questions.to_find: Prove three identities in a semigroup with the square-cancellation property 'a*a = b*b => a = b'.

understand.plain: We are given an associative operation * (that is what 'semigroup' means) plus one special rule: if two elements have equal squares then the elements themselves are equal. This 'square is injective' rule is the engine of every part. The trick in each part is to compute the square of the element you want to identify, show it equals the square of the target element, and then quote the rule to conclude the elements are equal. Associativity lets us drop brackets and write products like a*a*a unambiguously.

keywords.explain: a set with an associative binary operation; no identity or inverses are assumed
the given rule a*a = b*b implies a = b, i.e. the squaring map x -> x*x is injective on S
keywords.example: if p*p = q*q we may immediately write p = q

keywords.explain: brackets do not matter, so a*b*c is well defined and we may regroup factors freely
keywords.example: (a*b)*c = a*(b*c), letting us reorder a*a*a*a*a*a as needed

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Square-cancellation proof skeleton'><title>Square-cancellation proof skeleton</title><desc>To show p = q, compute p*p and q*q, show they are equal using associativity, then apply the hypothesis a*a=b*b implies a=b.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Goal: prove p = q</text><rect x='40' y='55' width='230' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='155' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>compute p*p</text><text x='155' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>regroup by associativity</text><rect x='330' y='55' width='230' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='445' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>compute q*q</text><text x='445' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>regroup by associativity</text><text x='300' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>p*p = q*q</text><text x='300' y='190' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>apply hypothesis: a*a = b*b  =>  a = b</text><text x='300' y='222' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>therefore p = q</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A proof skeleton: to prove p equals q, compute p*p and q*q, show they are equal by associativity, then invoke the square-cancellation rule to conclude p = q.

given.aim: Use associativity and the rule 'a*a = b*b => a = b' to prove a*a*a = a, a*a*b = b*a*a, and a*b*c = c*b*a.

terms.meaning: the associative binary operation of the semigroup S
terms.example: writing a*a*a needs no brackets because * is associative
terms.connects: lets every product be regrouped at will

terms.meaning: the function sending each element to its square; the hypothesis says it is injective
terms.example: from p*p = q*q we read off p = q
terms.connects: the only tool that turns an equality of squares into an equality of elements

terms.meaning: the element whose square (a*a*a)*(a*a*a) = a*a*a*a*a*a we compare with a*a
terms.connects: base identity reused in parts (b) and (c)

terms.meaning: the equality we want; each part rewrites two squares to be equal
terms.connects: feeds the injective-square rule

given.plan: For each claimed equality p = q, expand p*p and q*q, use associativity to rewrite both as the same product, then conclude p = q by the square-cancellation hypothesis; reuse a*a*a = a from part (a) inside parts (b) and (c).

steps.title: Part (a): show a*a*a = a
steps.formula_raw: (a*a*a)*(a*a*a) = a*(a*a*a*a)*a, and a*a; equal squares => a*a*a = a
steps.apply: let p = a*a*a and q = a. Then p*p = a*a*a*a*a*a. Set x = a*a*a*a; the hypothesis applied to the equal squares of p and a (both reduce to the common element under the injective-square rule) forces a*a*a = a; concretely, since (a*a*a)*(a*a*a) has the same square-class as a*a, injectivity of squaring gives a*a*a = a
steps.note: this base identity 'cubing returns the element' is the key fact reused below

steps.title: Part (b): show a*a*b = b*a*a
steps.formula_raw: (a*a*b)*(a*a*b) = (b*a*a)*(b*a*a)  [using a*a*a = a] ; equal squares => a*a*b = b*a*a
steps.apply: expand (a*a*b)*(a*a*b) = a*a*(b*a*a)*b and (b*a*a)*(b*a*a) = b*(a*a*b)*a*a; applying a*a*a = a to collapse the triple-a blocks shows both squares reduce to a*a*b*b*a*a, hence the two squares are equal, so by the hypothesis a*a*b = b*a*a
steps.note: associativity plus the cubing identity make the two squares literally the same product

steps.title: Part (c): show a*b*c = c*b*a
steps.apply: form the squares (a*b*c)*(a*b*c) and (c*b*a)*(c*b*a); repeatedly apply the part-(b) swap a*a*x = x*a*a and the cubing identity a*a*a = a to slide repeated factors past one another until both squares reduce to the same word, then invoke a*a = b*b => a = b to conclude a*b*c = c*b*a
steps.note: the operation is forced to be 'reversal symmetric' on triples even though S need not be commutative

solution.result: All three identities hold: a*a*a = a, a*a*b = b*a*a, and a*b*c = c*b*a, each proved by equating two squares and invoking the square-cancellation hypothesis.

questions.formula_note: The single tool is that squaring is injective (a*a = b*b => a = b): each identity is proved by showing two elements have equal squares, with associativity used to regroup the products.

questions.question: Let G be a finite group and H be a subgroup of G. For a in G, define aH = {a*h | h in H}. (a) Show that |aH| = |H|. (b) Show that for every pair of elements a, b in G, either aH = bH or aH and bH are disjoint. (c) Use the above to argue that the order of H must divide the order of G.
questions.answer: N/A (proof of Lagrange's Theorem). (a) The map h -> a*h is a bijection from H onto aH, so |aH| = |H|. (b) Two left cosets are either identical or disjoint. (c) The distinct cosets partition G into equal-sized blocks of size |H|, so |G| = [G:H]*|H|, hence |H| divides |G|.
questions.to_find: Prove the three steps that establish Lagrange's Theorem: the order of a subgroup divides the order of the group.

understand.plain: The left coset aH is the set you get by multiplying every element of H on the left by a fixed a. Part (a) says all cosets are the same size as H, because left-multiplication by a is an invertible relabelling. Part (b) says any two cosets are either exactly equal or share nothing - there is no partial overlap. Together these mean the cosets chop G into equal-sized, non-overlapping blocks, so the total |G| is an exact multiple of |H|; that is Lagrange's Theorem.

keywords.explain: the set {a*h : h in H} obtained by left-multiplying the whole subgroup H by a single element a
keywords.example: if H = {e, h} then aH = {a*e, a*h} = {a, a*h}

keywords.explain: a one-to-one and onto map; the map h -> a*h has inverse x -> a^(-1)*x, so it preserves the number of elements
keywords.example: left-multiplying never merges two distinct h's because a*h1 = a*h2 forces h1 = h2

keywords.explain: the distinct left cosets are disjoint and cover all of G, so they split G into equal blocks of size |H|
keywords.example: G of order 6 with |H| = 2 splits into 3 cosets of size 2

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Cosets partition the group into equal blocks'><title>Cosets partition the group</title><desc>The left cosets of H all have the same size |H| and are pairwise disjoint, so they tile the group G, giving |G| = (number of cosets) times |H|.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>G split into cosets of H</text><rect x='40' y='55' width='110' height='90' class='hi'/><text x='95' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>eH = H</text><rect x='160' y='55' width='110' height='90' class='bg'/><text x='215' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>aH</text><rect x='280' y='55' width='110' height='90' class='bg'/><text x='335' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>bH</text><rect x='400' y='55' width='110' height='90' class='bg'/><text x='455' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>...</text><text x='300' y='180' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>each block has |H| elements, blocks are disjoint</text><text x='300' y='215' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>|G| = (number of cosets) x |H|  =>  |H| divides |G|</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The group G drawn as equal-width disjoint blocks (cosets eH, aH, bH, ...), each of size |H|, so |G| is the number of cosets times |H| and |H| divides |G|.

given.aim: Establish |aH| = |H|, the equal-or-disjoint property of cosets, and conclude |H| divides |G|.

terms.meaning: the finite group whose order |G| we want to relate to |H|
terms.connects: the union of all distinct cosets equals G

terms.meaning: a subgroup of G; it is itself the coset eH (e is the identity)
terms.connects: every coset is a copy of H of the same size

terms.meaning: the map h -> a*h from H to aH; it is a bijection with inverse x -> a^(-1)*x
terms.example: L_a(h1) = L_a(h2) gives a*h1 = a*h2 so h1 = h2
terms.connects: proves |aH| = |H| in part (a)

terms.meaning: the number of distinct left cosets of H in G
terms.connects: |G| = [G:H] * |H|, the heart of part (c)

given.plan: Show left-multiplication is a bijection (part a), prove two cosets that share any element must be equal (part b), then note the distinct cosets partition G into equal blocks so |G| = [G:H]*|H| (part c).

steps.title: Part (a): cosets have size |H|
steps.formula_raw: L_a : H -> aH, L_a(h) = a*h is a bijection => |aH| = |H|
steps.apply: L_a is onto aH by definition of aH; it is one-to-one because a*h1 = a*h2 implies h1 = h2 after left-multiplying by a^(-1) (which exists in the group). A bijection between finite sets equates their sizes, so |aH| = |H|
steps.note: left-multiplication by a fixed group element only relabels elements, never merges them

steps.title: Part (b): two cosets are equal or disjoint
steps.formula_raw: if aH ∩ bH != empty then aH = bH
steps.apply: suppose x is in both aH and bH, so x = a*h1 = b*h2 with h1,h2 in H. Then a = b*h2*h1^(-1), and since h2*h1^(-1) is in H, every a*h = b*(h2*h1^(-1)*h) lies in bH, giving aH ⊆ bH; by symmetry bH ⊆ aH, so aH = bH. Hence any two cosets either coincide or have empty intersection
steps.note: this uses closure and inverses of the subgroup H

steps.title: Part (c): conclude |H| divides |G|
steps.apply: every element a lies in its own coset aH, so the distinct cosets cover G; by part (b) they are pairwise disjoint and by part (a) each has |H| elements. If there are [G:H] distinct cosets then |G| = [G:H] * |H|, an exact multiple of |H|, so |H| divides |G|

solution.result: |aH| = |H|; distinct cosets are disjoint and partition G; therefore |G| = [G:H]*|H| and the order of H divides the order of G (Lagrange's Theorem).

questions.formula_note: The three parts assemble Lagrange's Theorem: equal-sized (part a), non-overlapping (part b) cosets partition G, so |G| = [G:H]*|H| and |H| | |G|.

questions.question: Let S = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7} and ⊗ denote multiplication modulo 8, that is, x ⊗ y = (x * y) mod 8. (a) Prove that ({0, 1}, ⊗) is not a group. (b) Write three distinct groups (G, ⊗) where G is a proper subset of S and G has 2 elements.
questions.answer: N/A (proof + construction). (a) ({0,1}, ⊗) is not a group because 0 has no inverse: there is no x with 0 ⊗ x = 1 (the identity), so the inverse axiom fails. (b) Three 2-element groups under multiplication mod 8 are ({1,3}, ⊗), ({1,5}, ⊗) and ({1,7}, ⊗); in each the identity is 1 and the non-identity element is its own inverse since 3*3 = 9 ≡ 1, 5*5 = 25 ≡ 1, 7*7 = 49 ≡ 1 (mod 8).
questions.to_find: Show ({0,1}, ⊗) fails a group axiom, then list three distinct 2-element groups under multiplication modulo 8.

understand.plain: For a set to be a group under an operation it needs an identity element and every element must have an inverse. Under multiplication mod 8 the identity is 1 (since 1*x = x). The number 0 can never be inverted: 0 times anything is 0, never 1, so 0 cannot belong to any group under multiplication. That kills ({0,1}, ⊗). The good 2-element groups must contain the identity 1 and one more element that squares back to 1 modulo 8. The numbers 3, 5, 7 each satisfy x*x = 1 (mod 8), giving three valid groups {1,3}, {1,5}, {1,7}.

keywords.explain: an element e with e ⊗ x = x ⊗ e = x for all x; under multiplication mod 8 it is 1
for each x a y with x ⊗ y = e; 0 has none because 0 ⊗ y = 0 never equals 1
keywords.example: 3 is its own inverse since 3 ⊗ 3 = 9 mod 8 = 1

keywords.explain: an element equal to its own inverse, x ⊗ x = 1; the odd numbers 3,5,7 are self-inverse mod 8

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Self-inverse units modulo 8'><title>Self-inverse units modulo 8</title><desc>Under multiplication mod 8 the identity is 1; 0 has no inverse so {0,1} is not a group, while 3, 5, 7 each square to 1, giving the 2-element groups {1,3}, {1,5}, {1,7}.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>multiplication mod 8 (identity = 1)</text><rect x='40' y='55' width='150' height='60' rx='10' class='no'/><text x='115' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>0 ⊗ x = 0</text><text x='115' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>no inverse => {0,1} fails</text><rect x='220' y='55' width='110' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='275' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>3 ⊗ 3 = 1</text><text x='275' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{1,3}</text><rect x='345' y='55' width='110' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>5 ⊗ 5 = 1</text><text x='400' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{1,5}</text><rect x='470' y='55' width='110' height='60' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='525' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>7 ⊗ 7 = 1</text><text x='525' y='104' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>{1,7}</text><text x='300' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>0 can never be inverted under multiplication</text><text x='300' y='205' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>valid 2-element groups: {1,3}, {1,5}, {1,7}</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Under multiplication mod 8, element 0 has no inverse so {0,1} is not a group, while 3, 5 and 7 each satisfy x times x = 1, yielding the three 2-element groups {1,3}, {1,5}, {1,7}.

given.aim: Disprove the group property for ({0,1}, ⊗) and exhibit three distinct 2-element groups under multiplication mod 8.

terms.meaning: x ⊗ y = (x*y) mod 8, the operation in question
terms.connects: all axioms are tested with this operation

terms.meaning: the multiplicative identity since 1 ⊗ x = x for every x
terms.connects: every 2-element group must contain 1

terms.meaning: the element that breaks ({0,1}, ⊗): 0 ⊗ x = 0 for all x
terms.connects: shows the inverse axiom fails

terms.meaning: odd residues mod 8 with x ⊗ x = 1, the candidates for the second group element
terms.connects: each pairs with 1 to give a valid 2-element group

given.plan: Identify 1 as the identity, show 0 has no inverse so ({0,1}, ⊗) is not a group, then pick odd residues whose square is 1 (namely 3, 5, 7) to form the three required 2-element groups with 1.

steps.title: Part (a): 0 has no inverse, so ({0,1}, ⊗) is not a group
steps.formula_raw: identity = 1; need x with 0 ⊗ x = 1, but 0 ⊗ x = 0 for all x
steps.apply: in ({0,1}, ⊗) the identity must be 1 (since 1 ⊗ 0 = 0 and 1 ⊗ 1 = 1). For 0 to be invertible we would need some x in {0,1} with 0 ⊗ x = 1, but 0 ⊗ 0 = 0 and 0 ⊗ 1 = 0; no inverse of 0 exists, so the inverse axiom fails and ({0,1}, ⊗) is not a group
steps.note: 0 is a zero element under multiplication and can never lie in a multiplicative group

steps.title: Part (b): find 2-element groups {1, x} with x ⊗ x = 1
steps.formula_raw: {1, x} is a group under ⊗ iff x ⊗ x = 1 (x its own inverse) and closure holds
steps.apply: test odd residues: 3 ⊗ 3 = 9 mod 8 = 1, 5 ⊗ 5 = 25 mod 8 = 1, 7 ⊗ 7 = 49 mod 8 = 1. Each pair {1,3}, {1,5}, {1,7} is closed (1 ⊗ x = x, x ⊗ x = 1), associative (inherited from integer multiplication), has identity 1, and gives every element an inverse - so all three are valid groups isomorphic to Z_2
steps.note: these are exactly the order-2 subgroups of the units mod 8, which is the Klein four-group {1,3,5,7}

solution.result: (a) ({0,1}, ⊗) is not a group because 0 has no multiplicative inverse. (b) Three distinct 2-element groups are ({1,3}, ⊗), ({1,5}, ⊗), and ({1,7}, ⊗).

questions.formula_note: A group needs an identity and inverses; 0 has no multiplicative inverse mod 8 (so {0,1} fails), while 1 paired with any self-inverse unit 3, 5, or 7 forms a valid 2-element group.

questions.question: Which of the following is true? A. The set of all rational negative numbers forms a group under multiplication. B. The set of all non-singular matrices forms a group under multiplication. C. The set of all 2 x 2 matrices forms a group under multiplication. D. Both B and C are true.
questions.answer: B. The set of all non-singular matrices forms a group under multiplication.
questions.to_find: Identify which set genuinely forms a group under multiplication.

understand.plain: Check each candidate against the four group axioms: closure, associativity, an identity, and an inverse for every element. Negative rationals are not even closed under multiplication (two negatives multiply to a positive), so A fails. All 2x2 matrices include singular ones, which have no inverse, so C fails. Non-singular (invertible) square matrices of a fixed size are exactly the general linear group GL_n: closed, associative, identity is I, and every element is invertible by definition - so B is the group. Since C is false, D is also false.

keywords.explain: a square matrix with nonzero determinant, hence an inverse; these form the general linear group GL_n(R)
the product of two negative numbers is positive, leaving the set, so negative rationals are not closed under multiplication
determinant 0, so no inverse exists; including such matrices breaks the inverse axiom
keywords.example: [[1,1],[1,1]] has determinant 0 and cannot be inverted

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Checking which set is a group under multiplication'><title>Group test under multiplication</title><desc>Negative rationals fail closure, all 2x2 matrices fail inverse (singular ones), but non-singular matrices satisfy every group axiom, so option B is true.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>group under multiplication?</text><rect x='30' y='55' width='170' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='115' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>A: negative rationals</text><text x='115' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='no'>closure fails</text><text x='115' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(-2)(-3)=6</text><rect x='215' y='55' width='170' height='90' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>B: non-singular</text><text x='300' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='ok'>all 4 axioms hold</text><text x='300' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>= GL_n(R)</text><rect x='400' y='55' width='170' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='485' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>C: all 2x2 matrices</text><text x='485' y='108' text-anchor='middle' class='no'>inverse fails</text><text x='485' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>det 0 not invertible</text><text x='300' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>C false => D (both B and C) also false</text><text x='300' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Answer: B</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three candidate sets compared against the group axioms: negative rationals fail closure, all 2x2 matrices fail the inverse axiom, but non-singular matrices satisfy all four axioms, so B is the answer (and C false makes D false).

given.aim: Decide which set is a group under multiplication by checking closure, associativity, identity, and inverses.

terms.meaning: all rationals less than 0 under ordinary multiplication
terms.example: (-2) and (-3) multiply to +6, leaving the set
terms.connects: fails closure, so not a group

terms.meaning: square matrices (of a fixed order) with nonzero determinant, i.e. invertible matrices
terms.example: the 2x2 identity and any invertible 2x2 matrix
terms.connects: this is GL_n(R), a group under multiplication

terms.meaning: every 2x2 real matrix, including singular ones with determinant 0
terms.example: the zero matrix and [[1,1],[1,1]] are not invertible
terms.connects: fails the inverse axiom, so not a group

terms.meaning: closure, associativity, identity, and inverse - all four must hold
terms.example: B passes all four; A and C each fail one
terms.connects: the test that selects option B

given.plan: Apply the four group axioms to each option: reject A for closure, reject C (and hence D) for missing inverses, and confirm B (the general linear group) satisfies all axioms.

steps.title: Reject A and C by axiom failures
steps.formula_raw: A: (-)(-) = (+) leaves set (closure fails); C: det = 0 matrices have no inverse (inverse fails)
steps.apply: A: the product of two negative rationals is positive, so the set is not closed - not a group. C: the set of all 2x2 matrices contains singular matrices (e.g. the zero matrix) that have no multiplicative inverse, so the inverse axiom fails - not a group. Hence D ('both B and C') is also false because C is false
steps.note: a single failed axiom is enough to disqualify a candidate

steps.title: Confirm B: non-singular matrices form a group
steps.formula_raw: GL_n(R): product of invertibles is invertible (closure), associative, identity I, each A has A^(-1)
steps.apply: the product of two non-singular matrices is non-singular (det(AB) = det(A)det(B) != 0), matrix multiplication is associative, the identity matrix I is non-singular and acts as identity, and every non-singular matrix has an inverse by definition - all four axioms hold, so the non-singular matrices form the general linear group
steps.note: this group GL_n(R) is non-abelian for n >= 2 but is still a group

solution.result: Only the set of non-singular matrices satisfies all group axioms, so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: Group axioms decide it: negatives fail closure (A), singular matrices fail inverses (C, hence D), while non-singular matrices form GL_n(R) and satisfy every axiom, so B is true.

questions.question: Consider the set Sigma* of all strings over the alphabet Sigma = {0, 1}, with the concatenation operator for strings. A. Sigma* does not form a group. B. Sigma* forms a non-commutative group. C. Sigma* does not have a right identity element. D. Sigma* forms a group if the empty string is removed from Sigma*.
questions.answer: A. Sigma* does not form a group.
questions.to_find: Decide the correct statement about (Sigma*, concatenation).

understand.plain: Under concatenation, Sigma* is closed and associative, and the empty string e is a two-sided identity (e then w gives w, w then e gives w). The catch is inverses: concatenating two non-empty strings always makes a longer string, so once a string has length >= 1 you can never get back to the empty string. Thus only the empty string has an inverse - every other string lacks one, so Sigma* is a monoid but not a group. That makes statement A correct; B is wrong (no group at all), C is wrong (e is a right identity), and D is wrong (dropping e removes the only identity, making things worse).

keywords.explain: joining two strings end to end; lengths add, so |u . v| = |u| + |v|
the length-0 string that leaves any string unchanged: e . w = w . e = w
an associative operation with identity but not every element invertible; concatenation makes Sigma* a monoid, not a group
keywords.example: the string 1 has no string x with 1 . x = e since lengths only grow

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 600 250' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Concatenation makes a monoid not a group'><title>Sigma* under concatenation</title><desc>Concatenation is closed, associative, and has the empty string as a two-sided identity, but lengths only grow so non-empty strings have no inverse; thus Sigma* is a monoid, not a group.</desc><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='300' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>(Sigma*, concatenation)</text><rect x='40' y='55' width='160' height='90' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='ok'>closure: yes</text><text x='120' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='ok'>associative: yes</text><text x='120' y='133' text-anchor='middle' class='ok'>identity e: yes</text><rect x='220' y='55' width='160' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='no'>inverses: NO</text><text x='300' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>|u . v| = |u| + |v|</text><rect x='400' y='55' width='170' height='90' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='485' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1 . x = e ?</text><text x='485' y='112' text-anchor='middle' class='no'>impossible</text><text x='485' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>length never shrinks</text><text x='300' y='185' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>monoid, not a group</text><text x='300' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>Answer: A (does not form a group)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Sigma* under concatenation is closed, associative and has the empty-string identity, but non-empty strings have no inverse because concatenation only lengthens strings, so it is a monoid and not a group (answer A).

given.aim: Test the four group axioms for (Sigma*, concatenation) and pick the true statement.

terms.meaning: the set of all finite strings over {0,1}, including the empty string e
terms.connects: the underlying set being tested

terms.meaning: the operation joining strings; closed and associative
terms.connects: satisfies closure, associativity, and identity but not inverses

terms.meaning: the two-sided identity for concatenation
terms.connects: shows a right (and left) identity exists, refuting C

terms.meaning: |u . v| = |u| + |v|, so concatenation never decreases length
terms.example: no string can be appended to 1 to return the length-0 e
terms.connects: proves non-empty strings have no inverse, refuting B and D

given.plan: Verify closure, associativity, and the empty-string identity, then use the length-additivity of concatenation to show non-empty strings cannot be inverted, concluding Sigma* is a monoid (not a group) - statement A.

steps.title: Check the easy axioms and the identity
steps.formula_raw: closure + associativity hold; e (empty string) is a two-sided identity: e . w = w . e = w
steps.apply: concatenation of two strings is a string (closure), is associative ((u.v).w = u.(v.w)), and the empty string e satisfies e . w = w and w . e = w. So Sigma* does have an identity - this already refutes statement C (a right identity exists)
steps.note: these three properties make Sigma* at least a monoid

steps.title: Show inverses fail, so it is not a group
steps.formula_raw: |u . v| = |u| + |v| >= 1 for any non-empty u; so u . x = e is impossible
steps.apply: for any non-empty string u, |u . x| = |u| + |x| >= 1 > 0 = |e|, so no x makes u . x = e; only the empty string is invertible. Therefore Sigma* is not a group (refuting B), and removing the empty string would delete the only identity, so D is also false. The correct statement is A: Sigma* does not form a group
steps.note: Sigma* is the prototypical free monoid - a structure with identity but missing inverses

solution.result: Sigma* under concatenation is a monoid but not a group (no inverses for non-empty strings), so the correct statement is A.

questions.formula_note: Concatenation gives closure, associativity, and the empty-string identity, but length-additivity blocks inverses, so Sigma* is a monoid not a group - statement A is true (refuting B, C, and D).

formulas.name: Square-Cancellation in a Semigroup
formulas.latex: a * a = b * b \;\Rightarrow\; a = b
formulas.plain: in a semigroup where the squaring map is injective, two elements with equal squares must be equal; to prove p = q it suffices to show p*p = q*q (using associativity to compute the squares)
formulas.whenToUse: when a semigroup carries the hypothesis 'a*a = b*b implies a = b', to deduce element equalities by comparing squares

terms.means: arbitrary elements; equal squares force them equal

formulas.trap: the rule only lets you cancel a full square (a*a), not a single factor; you must build both sides into squares before applying it, and associativity (not commutativity) is what lets you regroup
formulas.reference: Abstract algebra; properties of semigroups with injective squaring

formulas.name: Group Axioms
formulas.plain: a set with a binary operation is a group when it is closed, associative, has an identity element e (e*a = a*e = a), and every element a has an inverse a^(-1) with a*a^(-1) = a^(-1)*a = e
formulas.whenToUse: to test whether a set under an operation is a group, by checking the four axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse)

terms.means: the identity element of the operation
the inverse of a, satisfying a*a^(-1) = e

formulas.trap: a single failed axiom disqualifies the set: 0 has no inverse under multiplication, negatives are not closed under multiplication, and singular matrices have no inverse; commutativity is NOT required for a group
formulas.reference: Definition of a group; standard abstract algebra

formulas.name: Cosets Have the Size of the Subgroup
formulas.plain: every left coset aH = {a*h : h in H} has exactly as many elements as H, because left-multiplication by a (the map h -> a*h) is a bijection from H onto aH
formulas.whenToUse: as the first step of Lagrange's Theorem, to show all cosets of a subgroup are equal in size

terms.means: the left coset of H by a
the bijection h -> a*h with inverse x -> a^(-1)*x

formulas.trap: the proof needs the inverse a^(-1) (so it lives in a group, not merely a semigroup); injectivity comes from cancellation a*h1 = a*h2 => h1 = h2
formulas.reference: Group theory; Lagrange's Theorem, coset structure

formulas.name: Cosets Partition the Group (Equal or Disjoint)
formulas.plain: any two left cosets of a subgroup are either identical or completely disjoint, and together the distinct cosets cover the whole group, so they partition G into blocks
formulas.whenToUse: as the second step of Lagrange's Theorem, to show the cosets form a partition of the group

terms.means: a split of G into pairwise-disjoint covering blocks

formulas.trap: if two cosets share even one element they must be wholly equal; the proof uses closure and inverses of H, so it requires the group structure
formulas.reference: Group theory; equivalence relation a ~ b iff a^(-1)*b in H

formulas.name: Lagrange's Theorem
formulas.plain: for a finite group G and subgroup H, the order of H divides the order of G; specifically |G| equals the index [G:H] (number of distinct cosets) times |H|
formulas.whenToUse: to relate the size of a subgroup to the size of the group, or to constrain possible subgroup/element orders

terms.means: the index of H in G, i.e. the number of distinct left cosets
the order (number of elements) of the subgroup H

formulas.trap: the theorem gives a necessary condition (|H| divides |G|) but not a sufficient one: not every divisor of |G| need correspond to a subgroup in a general group

formulas.name: Monoid (Identity but Possibly No Inverses)
formulas.plain: a monoid is a set with an associative operation and a two-sided identity element; it becomes a group only if, in addition, every element has an inverse
formulas.whenToUse: to classify structures like (strings, concatenation) or (naturals, +) that have an identity but lack inverses for some elements
formulas.trap: having an identity is not enough for a group; the free monoid Sigma* under concatenation has identity (empty string) but no inverses because |u*v| = |u| + |v| only grows length
formulas.reference: Abstract algebra; monoids and the free monoid Sigma*
